707-320: The Boeing 707-320 is a four-engine narrow-body airliner, identifiable by its turbojet engines mounted in pods under a swept-back wing.
A key feature of the 707-320 is its quartet of low-bypass engines, which are noticeably slimmer and longer than modern high-bypass turbofans.
The 707-320 can be recognized by its distinctive HF antenna protruding forward from the top of its vertical stabilizer.
From the side, the Boeing 707-320 has a long, slender fuselage and a conventional tail design.
Unlike later jets, the wings of the 707-320 typically lack winglets or any other wingtip devices.
The cockpit windows of the 707-320 have a classic six-pane design, characteristic of early Boeing jetliners.
The Boeing 707-320 is a pioneering long-haul jet, setting the design standard with its four underwing engines.
An aerial view of the 707-320 shows its significant wingspan and the four distinct engine pods.
The tail of the 707-320 features a prominent bulge at the base of the rudder, housing a fuel tank in some versions.
As one of the first successful jet airliners, the 707-320 has a vintage appearance with its polished metal or classic airline liveries.
727-200: The Boeing 727-200 is a trijet airliner easily identified by its three rear-mounted engines and a distinctive T-tail.
A key spotting feature of the 727-200 is its central engine, which is integrated into the rear fuselage with an S-shaped duct.
The 727-200 is a stretched version of the original, resulting in a noticeably long and slender fuselage for a narrow-body jet.
The T-tail configuration of the Boeing 727-200, with the horizontal stabilizers on top of the vertical fin, is its most defining characteristic.
Unlike underwing-engine aircraft, the 727-200 has clean, uncluttered wings that are swept back.
The Boeing 727-200 also features a unique retractable aft airstair, a ventral staircase at the rear of the fuselage.
The sound of the 727-200 is distinctive due to its three low-bypass Pratt & Whitney JT8D engines.
From the front, the 727-200 has a classic Boeing cockpit window arrangement and a high ground clearance.
The Boeing 727-200 can be distinguished from the shorter -100 model by its greater length and additional cabin windows.
As a trijet, the 727-200's unique engine layout with one engine on each side of the rear fuselage and one in the tail is unmistakable.
737-200: The Boeing 737-200 is an early generation twinjet, recognized by its long, thin, cigar-shaped low-bypass turbojet engines.
A defining feature of the 737-200 is that its engines are mounted directly to the underside of the wings, not on pylons.
The 737-200 has a relatively short fuselage and a tail fin that is shorter and more rounded than later 737 variants.
Unlike modern 737s, the 737-200 has no winglets and a simple, tapered wingtip.
The cockpit of the Boeing 737-200 features the classic six-pane window design and often 'eyebrow' windows above the main windshield.
The 737-200 is known for its loud and smoky Pratt & Whitney JT8D engines, a key auditory identification feature.
From the side, the short body and long, thin engines give the Boeing 737-200 a unique and somewhat dated profile.
The 737-200 often operated on gravel runways, and some were fitted with a gravel kit, including a distinctive nose-gear ski.
The tailcone of the 737-200 is typically more pointed compared to the blunter APU exhaust of later models.
As a first-generation 737, the 737-200's overall appearance is characterized by its short stature and vintage engine design.
737-300: The Boeing 737-300 is a 'Classic' series twinjet, identified by its larger high-bypass engines with a distinctive flattened, non-circular bottom on the nacelle.
This flattened engine shape on the 737-300, often called a 'hamster pouch', was necessary due to the aircraft's low ground clearance.
The fuselage of the 737-300 is longer than the -200 and -500, but shorter than the -400.
The Boeing 737-300 has a more refined wing design than its predecessor and can sometimes be seen with small blended winglets.
Compared to the 737-200, the 737-300 has a taller and more angular vertical stabilizer.
The 737-300 has a single over-wing emergency exit door, which helps distinguish it from the longer 737-400.
The engines on the Boeing 737-300 (CFM56-3) are positioned further forward on the wing than on the 737-200.
From the front, the non-circular intake of the engines is the most prominent identifying feature of the 737-300.
The 737-300's side profile shows a balanced narrow-body jet, a staple of short-haul travel in its era.
As part of the 737 Classic family, the 737-300 represents a visual bridge between the original and the Next Generation models.
737-400: The Boeing 737-400 is the longest member of the 737 'Classic' family, a stretched version of the 737-300.
Its most reliable visual identifier is the presence of two over-wing emergency exit doors on each side of the fuselage.
The 737-400 shares the distinctive flattened 'hamster pouch' engine nacelles of the CFM56-3 engines with the rest of the Classic series.
The extended length of the 737-400's fuselage is clearly noticeable when compared to the -300 or -500 models.
The Boeing 737-400 was designed for higher capacity routes, and its elongated shape reflects this role.
Like other 737 Classics, the 737-400 has a more angular tail and cockpit design than the later Next Generation series.
From a distance, counting the two over-wing exits is the quickest way to confirm you are looking at a 737-400.
The wings of the 737-400 are essentially the same as the -300, with simple tips or occasionally retrofitted winglets.
The landing gear of the Boeing 737-400 includes a tail skid at the rear of the fuselage to protect against tail strikes during takeoff.
The 737-400's profile is that of a stretched, workhorse narrow-body with the characteristic flat-bottomed engines of its generation.
737-500: The Boeing 737-500 is the shortest member of the 737 'Classic' family, essentially a direct replacement for the 737-200.
It is easily identified by its short, 'stubby' fuselage combined with the modern, flat-bottomed CFM56 engines of the Classic series.
The 737-500 has a single over-wing emergency exit door, similar to the 737-300 but on a much shorter body.
Its fuselage length is almost identical to the 737-200, but the 737-500 is distinguished by its quieter, more efficient high-bypass engines.
The Boeing 737-500 combines the short-field performance of the -200 with the modern technology of the -300/-400.
The combination of a short body and relatively large, modern engines gives the 737-500 a powerful, compact appearance.
Compared to the later 737-600, the 737-500 is distinguished by its non-circular, flattened engine nacelles.
The 737-500 is essentially a 737-300 with a fuselage section removed from both forward and aft of the wing.
Visually, the Boeing 737-500 looks like a shortened version of the much more common 737-300.
Its profile is that of a short, robust jet with the characteristic 'hamster pouch' engines, optimized for short routes.
737-600: The Boeing 737-600 is the shortest member of the 737 'Next Generation' (NG) family, notable for its short fuselage and modern features.
A key identifier of the 737-600 is its perfectly round engine nacelles, a signature of the NG series, on a short body.
The 737-600 is unique among the NG family for almost never being fitted with winglets, featuring a simple tapered wingtip.
It has the taller, more angular vertical stabilizer and rudder characteristic of all 737 NG aircraft.
The 737-600 has a fuselage length similar to the 737-500 but is distinguished by its large, perfectly circular engines.
The Boeing 737-600 has a single over-wing emergency exit, consistent with its shorter length.
Its side profile is that of a compact, modern jet, but its relative rarity makes it an interesting spot.
Compared to the A318, another short airliner, the 737-600 has a more pointed tailcone and different cockpit window design.
The combination of the modern, round NG engines and a short body gives the Boeing 737-600 a powerful, almost sporty look.
As the direct replacement for the 737-500, the 737-600's main visual update is the circular engine intakes and lack of 'eyebrow' windows.
737-700: The Boeing 737-700 is the baseline model of the 'Next Generation' family, with a medium-length fuselage.
It is identified by its perfectly round engine nacelles and, most commonly, the presence of large blended winglets at the wingtips.
The 737-700 has a single over-wing emergency exit, which helps distinguish it from the longer 737-800.
This aircraft features the taller, more angular vertical tail characteristic of the 737 NG series.
The Boeing 737-700 has a balanced profile, serving as the direct replacement for the popular 737-300.
Its round CFM56-7 engines are larger and more powerful than the engines on the 737 Classic series.
Visually comparing the 737-700 to the Airbus A319, the 737 has a more pointed nose and a fin that is not faired into the fuselage.
The presence of blended winglets is a very strong, though not universal, indicator of a 737-700.
The 737-700's fuselage appears proportional to its wings and tail, making it the archetypal look of the 737 NG family.
From the front, the Boeing 737-700 is recognized by its V-shaped cockpit windows and large, circular engine intakes.
737-800: The Boeing 737-800 is a stretched version of the 737-700 and the most common variant of the 737 NG family.
Its most reliable spotting feature is the presence of two over-wing emergency exit doors on each side.
The 737-800 is almost always fitted with wingtip devices, either the classic blended winglets or the newer split scimitar winglets.
The aircraft has a noticeably long and slender fuselage, especially when compared to the 737-700.
The Boeing 737-800 has the signature round engine nacelles and tall angular tail of the NG series.
Its main landing gear features a more robust, complex design than earlier 737s to support the higher weight.
The 737-800 is a direct competitor to the Airbus A320, but can be distinguished by its pointed tailcone and 4-pane cockpit window design.
The combination of a long fuselage and two over-wing exits is the definitive way to identify a Boeing 737-800.
Its side profile is that of a long, modern narrow-body airliner, a common sight at airports worldwide.
The split scimitar winglet, with a downward-pointing ventral strake, is a very common and eye-catching feature on the 737-800.
737-900: The Boeing 737-900 is the longest variant of the 737 NG family, a further stretch of the 737-800.
Early 737-900 models have the same door layout as the -800, but the more common 737-900ER adds an extra pair of exit doors behind the wings.
The extreme length of the 737-900 fuselage is its most striking characteristic, appearing almost disproportionate.
Like the -800, the Boeing 737-900 almost always features blended or split scimitar winglets.
The 737-900ER model requires a two-position tail skid to protect its elongated rear fuselage from tail strikes.
This aircraft shares the round engine nacelles and tall angular tail of all other 737 NG models.
To differentiate a 737-900ER from a 737-800, look for the extra, small exit door just aft of the wing.
The 737-900 competes with the Airbus A321, and its length is comparable, though its fuselage is narrower.
The profile of the Boeing 737-900 is that of the longest, highest-capacity 737 ever produced.
Its visual identity is defined by its extreme length combined with the standard features of the 737 NG family.
747-100: The Boeing 747-100 is the original 'Jumbo Jet', immediately recognizable by its distinctive hump and four large engines.
A key feature of the 747-100 is its short upper deck, which typically has only three windows per side.
This aircraft is a wide-body, four-engine jet with a prominent swept-back wing.
The 747-100 was originally powered by early, less powerful turbofan engines compared to its successors.
The short, three-window hump is the most reliable way to distinguish the Boeing 747-100 from later passenger variants like the -300 and -400.
The cockpit is uniquely positioned on the upper deck, giving the nose of the 747 its iconic shape.
Like other early jets, the 747-100 has no winglets or wingtip extensions.
The sheer size of the Boeing 747-100, with its massive fuselage and four engines, defined an era of air travel.
The landing gear of the 747-100 is complex, with 18 wheels in total, including two four-wheel bogies under the fuselage.
The profile of the 747-100 is legendary: four engines, a massive wing, and the unmistakable short upper deck.
747-200: The Boeing 747-200 is a classic variant of the Jumbo Jet, visually very similar to the 747-100 but with more powerful engines.
It typically retains the short upper deck of the -100, usually with three or sometimes up to ten windows per side.
The most common way to distinguish a 747-200 from a -100 is often by the airline's fleet information, as visual cues are subtle.
The 747-200 is a four-engine wide-body with the iconic hump, but it offered increased range and payload over the -100.
Like the -100, the Boeing 747-200 does not have winglets on its wingtips.
Many 747-200s were converted to cargo aircraft, recognizable by a large side cargo door and often a windowless fuselage.
The four engine pods on the 747-200 house more powerful engines, which may appear slightly different from the original -100 powerplants.
The overall silhouette of the 747-200 is that of the classic Jumbo Jet: a massive fuselage, four engines, and the short, iconic hump.
The nose of the Boeing 747-200, with the cockpit on top, is one of the most recognizable designs in aviation.
Visually, the 747-200 is the quintessential classic 747, often seen in liveries from the golden age of jet travel.
747-300: The Boeing 747-300 is distinguished from earlier models by its stretched upper deck (SUD), which is a major visual upgrade.
The stretched upper deck of the 747-300 features a long, continuous line of windows, unlike the short deck of the -100 and -200.
The 747-300 retains the classic 747 wing without winglets, which differentiates it from the later 747-400.
This aircraft is a four-engine wide-body airliner, and its elongated hump gives it a more majestic profile.
The lack of winglets combined with the long upper deck is the definitive feature of a Boeing 747-300.
The internal staircase to the upper deck was changed from a spiral to a straight staircase in the 747-300.
From the side, the long, smooth curve of the stretched upper deck is the most prominent feature of the 747-300.
The engines of the 747-300 were more efficient than those on the -200, though visually similar.
The Boeing 747-300 served as a bridge between the classic 747s and the modern 747-400.
Its unique combination of a long upper deck and no winglets makes the 747-300 a relatively rare and interesting aircraft to spot.
747-400: The Boeing 747-400 is the most common and recognizable variant of the 747, combining the stretched upper deck with modern wings.
Its most definitive spotting feature is the presence of large, tall winglets at the end of its swept-back wings.
The 747-400 has the long, stretched upper deck (SUD) first introduced on the 747-300.
This aircraft is a four-engine, wide-body jet, and the winglets give it a more modern and efficient appearance.
The combination of a long upper deck and prominent winglets is the unmistakable signature of a Boeing 747-400.
The cockpit of the 747-400 is a two-crew glass cockpit, though this is not an external visual feature.
From a distance, the upward-canted winglets are the first and easiest way to identify a 747-400.
The Boeing 747-400 has a slightly extended wingspan compared to its predecessors, in addition to the winglets.
Its profile is that of the ultimate evolution of the classic Jumbo Jet, a common sight for long-haul international travel for decades.
The 747-400's iconic shape, defined by its hump, four engines, and winglets, makes it a true queen of the skies.
757-200: The Boeing 757-200 is a narrow-body twinjet known for its powerful appearance, often described as a 'sports car' of airliners.
It is easily identified by its long, slender fuselage, two large high-bypass engines, and a tall stance on its landing gear.
The 757-200 has a conventional tail design, but its vertical stabilizer is notably tall and large.
Many 757-200s have been retrofitted with large blended winglets, which accentuate its sleek appearance.
The aircraft has a very high thrust-to-weight ratio, allowing for impressive takeoff performance from short runways.
The nose and cockpit section of the Boeing 757-200 is shared with the wide-body 767, giving it a unique look for a narrow-body.
Its two powerful engines (either Rolls-Royce RB211 or Pratt & Whitney PW2000) are mounted under the wings on pylons.
The 757-200 has a distinctive 'pointy' nose shape and a high cockpit position.
Compared to a 737, the 757-200 sits much higher off the ground and has a noticeably longer, more pencil-like fuselage.
The overall impression of the Boeing 757-200 is one of power and grace, with its long body and large engines.
757-300: The Boeing 757-300 is the longest narrow-body twinjet ever built, a stretched version of the 757-200.
Its most defining characteristic is its extremely long and slender 'pencil-like' fuselage.
To accommodate the extra length and passenger capacity, the 757-300 has an additional pair of exit doors behind the wing.
Like the -200, the 757-300 often features large blended winglets, enhancing its long, sleek look.
The Boeing 757-300 sits very high off the ground on tall landing gear, a feature inherited from the -200.
It shares the same wing, engines, and tall vertical stabilizer as the 757-200.
The extreme length-to-width ratio of the 757-300 fuselage is its most unmistakable feature.
A retractable tail skid is fitted to the rear fuselage to protect against tail strikes on this very long aircraft.
The side profile of the Boeing 757-300 is unique in aviation, often nicknamed the 'Flying Pencil' for its proportions.
Identifying a 757-300 is easy: look for the characteristic 757 shape with an exceptionally long body and extra doors.
767-200: The Boeing 767-200 is a twin-engine wide-body airliner, the shortest variant in the 767 family.
It can be identified by its wide fuselage, two large high-bypass engines, and a relatively short body length for a wide-body.
The 767-200 shares its cockpit and nose design with the narrow-body 757, but its fuselage is significantly wider.
A key feature of the 767 family is the distinctive, somewhat pointed tailcone at the rear of the fuselage.
The Boeing 767-200 has two main landing gear bogies, each with four wheels, a standard for a wide-body of its size.
The aircraft's wings are swept back and, in later versions or retrofits, are often fitted with large blended winglets.
The side profile of the 767-200 is that of a compact, 'stubby' wide-body jet.
Compared to the A330, the 767-200 has a different cockpit window arrangement and a more pointed tailcone.
The Boeing 767-200's wide cabin is arranged in a 2-3-2 seating configuration, though this is not an external feature.
Its visual identity is that of the original, short-fuselage version of the successful 767 wide-body family.
767-300: The Boeing 767-300 is a stretched version of the 767-200 and the most common variant of the 767 family.
It is a twin-engine wide-body jet, and its fuselage is noticeably longer than the -200.
Many 767-300s are equipped with prominent blended winglets, which were introduced later in its production life.
The 767-300 has two main cabin doors forward of the wing and two exit doors over the wing on each side.
The aircraft features the same pointed tailcone and 757/767 common cockpit as the shorter -200.
The Boeing 767-300 has a balanced and proportional side profile, making it a very graceful-looking wide-body.
It is a versatile aircraft used for both passenger and cargo operations, with freighter versions having a large side cargo door.
The twin high-bypass engines of the 767-300 are powerful and relatively quiet for their generation.
Compared to the 767-400, the 767-300 is shorter and typically has winglets, whereas the -400 has raked wingtips.
The Boeing 767-300's combination of a medium-length wide-body, two engines, and often winglets defines its appearance.
767-400: The Boeing 767-400 is the longest variant of the 767 family, a further stretch of the 767-300.
Its most unique and reliable spotting feature is its raked wingtips, where the wingtip is extended and swept back more sharply.
The 767-400 does not have winglets; the raked wingtips serve the same aerodynamic purpose.
This aircraft is a twin-engine wide-body with a very long fuselage.
The main landing gear of the Boeing 767-400 is taller and features a new six-wheel bogie design, similar to the 777.
The fuselage of the 767-400 is noticeably longer than the more common -300 variant.
The absence of winglets and the presence of raked wingtips is the definitive way to identify a 767-400.
The cockpit windows of the 767-400 are updated and lack the 'eyebrow' windows of earlier 767s.
The Boeing 767-400 has a unique appearance due to its length and distinctive wingtips, making it a rare find.
Its side profile is that of a very long, stretched wide-body with two engines and sharply swept, extended wingtips.
777-200: The Boeing 777-200 is a large, twin-engine wide-body airliner, known for having the largest diameter turbofan engines in the world.
A key spotting feature is its massive engines, which are almost as wide as a 737's fuselage.
The 777-200's main landing gear is unique, with two bogies per side, each equipped with six wheels in a 3x2 configuration.
The rear of the aircraft features a distinctive blade-shaped or 'chisel' tailcone.
The Boeing 777-200 has a perfectly circular fuselage cross-section and a clean, advanced wing design.
Unlike the 767 or A330, the 777-200 has no winglets, featuring a simple raked wingtip on later models (like the -200ER/LR).
The cockpit windows of the 777-200 have a unique swept-back, angular design with six panes.
The sheer size of the two engines relative to the fuselage is a defining characteristic of the Boeing 777-200.
The six-wheel main landing gear bogies are the most reliable way to confirm an aircraft is a 777.
The 777-200's profile is that of a powerful, modern wide-body twin, the first of its kind.
777-300: The Boeing 777-300 is a dramatically stretched version of the 777-200, one of the longest aircraft in the world.
Its extreme fuselage length is its most obvious and defining characteristic.
The 777-300 retains the key features of the 777 family: two massive engines and the signature six-wheel main landing gear bogies.
Like the -200, it has a blade-shaped tailcone at the rear of the fuselage.
To protect its long fuselage, the 777-300 is equipped with a tail skid and ground-maneuvering cameras.
The Boeing 777-300 has five pairs of cabin doors, one more than the standard 777-200.
The more common 777-300ER variant is distinguished by its large, prominent raked wingtips.
The immense size of the engines on the 777-300, particularly the GE90 on the -300ER, is breathtaking.
Its side profile is that of a very long, majestic wide-body twinjet, a flagship for many airlines.
The combination of extreme length, two huge engines, and six-wheel landing gear makes the Boeing 777-300 unmistakable.
A300B4: The Airbus A300B4 is the world's first twin-engine wide-body airliner, a pioneering aircraft.
It is identified by its wide fuselage, two large high-bypass engines, and a distinctive cockpit window design.
The cockpit of the A300B4 has a unique six-window arrangement with curved, almost drooping side windows.
A key feature of the A300B4 is its relatively short and stout fuselage for a wide-body.
The wingtips of the A300B4 are simple, with no winglets, although some were later retrofitted with small triangular wingtip fences.
The tail of the Airbus A300B4 is large and conventional, with a smooth fairing where it joins the fuselage.
Its two engines are mounted under the wings on large pylons.
The landing gear of the A300B4 consists of two four-wheel bogies on the main gear.
Compared to the Boeing 767, the A300B4 has a wider, more bulbous fuselage and the characteristic Airbus cockpit.
The overall appearance of the Airbus A300B4 is that of a classic, first-generation wide-body twin.
A310: The Airbus A310 is a shortened, derivative of the A300, featuring a smaller but more advanced wing.
It is a twin-engine wide-body jet, but its fuselage is noticeably shorter and more compact than the A300.
A key visual difference of the A310 is its smaller, redesigned wing with a simpler flap system.
The A310 often features small, triangular wingtip fences, which were less common on the original A300.
The vertical tail of the Airbus A310 is also smaller than that of its predecessor.
The aircraft retains the characteristic Airbus six-window cockpit design and a wide, circular fuselage.
Its short and wide body gives the A310 a distinctively 'chubby' or stout appearance.
The two large turbofan engines are mounted under the wings on pylons.
From the side, the A310's short fuselage length relative to its tail height and wingspan is its most identifying feature.
The Airbus A310 is essentially a smaller, longer-range version of the A300, and its visual features reflect this heritage.
A318: The Airbus A318 is the shortest member of the A320 family, often nicknamed the 'Baby Bus'.
Its most defining feature is its short, 'stubby' fuselage combined with a disproportionately large vertical tail.
The A318 is a twin-engine narrow-body jet with the standard A320 family cockpit and fuselage cross-section.
This aircraft has only one over-wing emergency exit door on each side.
The tall vertical stabilizer on the A318 was necessary to provide adequate control for the short fuselage.
Some A318s are fitted with small wingtip fences, while others have the larger, modern sharklets.
Compared to the Boeing 737-600, the A318 has a blunter nose, a faired tail fin, and the larger tail.
The Airbus A318's combination of a very short body and a large tail gives it a unique and easily recognizable silhouette.
Its two engines (either CFM56 or PW6000) are mounted under the wings.
The A318 is a rare aircraft, and its short, tall-tailed appearance makes it a noteworthy spot.
A319: The Airbus A319 is a shortened version of the A320, with a well-proportioned and balanced appearance.
It is a twin-engine narrow-body airliner, identified by having a single over-wing emergency exit on each side.
The A319 shares the common cockpit, fuselage, and wing design of the A320 family.
Many A319s are equipped with the classic triangular wingtip fences, while newer ones feature large, curved sharklets.
The fuselage of the A319 is noticeably shorter than the A320, which has two over-wing exits.
Compared to the Boeing 737-700, the A319 has a blunter nose, a curved tail fin fairing, and a different cockpit window shape.
The Airbus A319's profile is that of a compact and versatile narrow-body jet.
Its two turbofan engines are mounted under the wings.
The single over-wing exit is the most reliable external feature to distinguish the A319 from the longer A320.
The overall look of the A319 is that of a quintessential modern short-to-medium-haul airliner.
A320: The Airbus A320 is the baseline model of its family and one of the world's most common airliners.
It is a twin-engine narrow-body jet, best identified by its two over-wing emergency exit doors on each side.
The A320 can be seen with either the original triangular wingtip fences or the newer, large, curved sharklets.
It features the characteristic Airbus blunted nose and the six-pane cockpit with its distinct side window shape.
The vertical tail of the A320 is smoothly faired into the rear fuselage, a common Airbus design trait.
Compared to its main rival, the Boeing 737-800, the A320 has a wider fuselage, a less pointed tailcone, and a different nose shape.
The Airbus A320 has a perfectly proportional side profile for a modern narrow-body aircraft.
Its two high-bypass turbofan engines are mounted under its swept-back wings.
The presence of two over-wing exits is the definitive feature that separates the A320 from the shorter A319.
The A320's appearance is the benchmark for a modern single-aisle jet, with either classic fences or modern sharklets at the wingtips.
A321: The Airbus A321 is the longest member of the A320 family, a stretched version of the A320.
Its most defining feature is its long, slender fuselage and its unique door configuration.
The A321 does not have over-wing exits; instead, it has four main cabin doors evenly spaced along each side of the fuselage.
This aircraft is a twin-engine narrow-body with the common A320 family features like the nose, cockpit, and tail design.
The lack of over-wing exits and the presence of four main doors is the unmistakable signature of an Airbus A321.
Like other A320 family members, the A321 can be found with either wingtip fences or the large, curved sharklets.
The A321's long fuselage required a strengthened landing gear compared to the A320.
Its side profile is that of a long, high-capacity narrow-body, a direct competitor to the Boeing 757.
The two turbofan engines of the A321 are more powerful to handle its increased weight.
The Airbus A321's unique door layout on its stretched fuselage makes it very easy to identify.
A330-200: The Airbus A330-200 is a twin-engine wide-body airliner, the shorter-fuselage variant of the A330 family.
It is characterized by its wide fuselage, two large engines, and a disproportionately large vertical tail fin.
The large tail fin on the A330-200 was designed for the longer A330-300, making it look oversized on the shorter body.
All A330 models feature prominent, tall winglets that are angled outwards.
The A330-200 shares the common Airbus flight deck and nose section with the A340.
Its side profile is that of a compact wide-body with a very tall tail, giving it a powerful stance.
The main landing gear of the Airbus A330-200 has a slight rearward tilt when on the ground.
Compared to the Boeing 767, the A330-200 has winglets (vs. raked tips or none) and the characteristic Airbus nose.
The combination of a shorter wide-body fuselage and a very large tail fin is the key to identifying an A330-200.
The two massive high-bypass turbofan engines complete the look of the Airbus A330-200.
A330-300: The Airbus A330-300 is a stretched, long-fuselage version of the A330 and the most common variant.
It is a twin-engine wide-body jet with a long, graceful fuselage that appears well-proportioned with its large tail fin.
Like the -200, the A330-300 is equipped with tall, angled winglets at the wingtips.
The fuselage of the A330-300 is noticeably longer than the -200, giving it a sleeker appearance.
The aircraft shares the Airbus wide-body family nose and cockpit design.
The main landing gear bogies of the A330-300 have a distinct rearward tilt on the ground.
Its two large engines are mounted under the swept-back wings.
The side profile of the Airbus A330-300 is that of a large, modern, and efficient long-haul airliner.
Compared to the four-engined A340-300 which shares the same fuselage, the A330-300 is a twinjet.
The combination of a long wide-body fuselage, two engines, and tall winglets is the definitive look of the A330-300.
A340-200: The Airbus A340-200 is a four-engine, long-range wide-body airliner, the shortest variant of the A340 family.
It is easily identified by its four engines on a wide-body fuselage that is relatively short, giving it a powerful look.
The A340-200 has the same fuselage length as a Boeing 767-300 but with four engines instead of two.
Like all A340s, it features tall, angled winglets at the wingtips.
The combination of four engines on a short wide-body is the unmistakable signature of an A340-200.
The Airbus A340-200 shares its wing and basic design with the twin-engine A330.
Its four engines are smaller in diameter compared to the large engines of twinjets like the A330 or 777.
The main landing gear includes a central, two-wheel bogie in addition to the two main four-wheel bogies.
The A340-200 is a very rare aircraft, making its unique short-body, four-engine configuration a special sight.
Its profile is stout and powerful, defined by having four engines on a compact wide-body airframe.
A340-300: The Airbus A340-300 is a four-engine, wide-body airliner, visually identical to the A330-300 but with four engines.
Its most defining characteristic is the presence of four underwing engines on a long, slender fuselage.
The A340-300 is equipped with the standard tall, angled Airbus winglets.
It shares the same fuselage, wing, and tail as the A330-300, with the number of engines being the key difference.
The four engines of the A340-300 are noticeably smaller in diameter than the two large engines of an A330.
The Airbus A340-300 has an additional two-wheel center main landing gear bogie to support the extra weight.
The side profile is that of a graceful, long-haul quad-jet, a classic design for intercontinental routes.
The quartet of sleek engines under the long wings gives the A340-300 a very balanced and symmetrical appearance.
Compared to a Boeing 747, the A340-300 is a single-deck aircraft without the characteristic hump.
The unmistakable look of the A340-300 is its long wide-body airframe with four engines and winglets.
A340-500: The Airbus A340-500 is an ultra-long-range variant, featuring the same fuselage length as the A340-300 but with a larger wing and engines.
It is a four-engine wide-body, but its engines (Rolls-Royce Trent 500) are noticeably larger in diameter than those on the -200/-300.
The A340-500 has a larger wingspan and a strengthened wing structure compared to the -300.
It retains the tall, angled winglets and the central main landing gear bogie of the A340 family.
The combination of four large engines on the shorter A340 fuselage (compared to the -600) is key to identifying the A340-500.
The fuselage of the A340-500 is long, but appears more powerful and less stretched than the A340-600.
The aircraft was designed for some of the world's longest air routes, reflected in its powerful four-engine design.
From the front, the larger engines and massive wingspan give the A340-500 an impressive stance.
The Airbus A340-500 is a rare aircraft, distinguished from the -300 primarily by its bigger engines.
Its profile is that of a high-performance quad-jet, optimized for range and power.
A340-600: The Airbus A340-600 is one of the world's longest airliners, a dramatically stretched version of the A340.
Its most defining feature is its exceptionally long and slender 'pencil-like' fuselage, combined with four engines.
The A340-600 has four large Rolls-Royce Trent 500 engines, the same as on the A340-500.
To support its immense length, the main landing gear includes a four-wheel central bogie, unlike the two-wheel bogie on shorter A340s.
The fuselage of the Airbus A340-600 is noticeably longer than a Boeing 747's.
It is equipped with the standard tall, angled Airbus winglets.
The side profile of the A340-600 is unmistakable due to its extreme length, making it look like a 'stretched limousine' of the skies.
The four-wheel center landing gear is a definitive feature to distinguish it from the A340-500.
The combination of an extremely long fuselage, four large engines, and winglets makes the A340-600 a unique and majestic sight.
The sheer length of the Airbus A340-600 is its single most impressive and identifiable characteristic.
A380: The Airbus A380 is the world's largest passenger airliner, immediately recognizable by its full-length twin-deck fuselage.
Its most defining feature is its massive, wide fuselage with two complete rows of windows one above the other.
The A380 is a four-engine, wide-body aircraft with a huge wingspan and a very large, swept-back wing.
The nose of the A380 is bulbous, with the cockpit situated between the two passenger decks.
The wings of the Airbus A380 are very large and are often tipped with simple raked wingtips or smaller wingtip fences.
Its landing gear is enormous, consisting of 22 wheels across five units, including two six-wheel bogies under the wings.
The vertical stabilizer of the A380 is immense, standing as tall as a medium-sized office building.
From any angle, the sheer scale and double-deck configuration of the A380 make it absolutely unmistakable.
Its four large engines appear almost small in proportion to the massive wing and fuselage.
The silhouette of the Airbus A380 is unique in aviation, defined by its massive size and two full passenger decks.
ATR-42: The ATR-42 is a twin-engine turboprop regional airliner, identified by its high-mounted wing and slender fuselage.
A key feature of the ATR-42 is its main landing gear, which is housed in prominent, unpressurized fairings on the lower fuselage sides.
This aircraft has a T-tail configuration, with the horizontal stabilizer mounted on top of the vertical fin.
The ATR-42 is the shorter version of the ATR family, giving it a somewhat compact and robust appearance.
Its two powerful turboprop engines are mounted on the high wing, with multi-bladed propellers.
The high wing design allows for good ground clearance for the propellers.
The side profile of the ATR-42 is characterized by its high wing, T-tail, and the distinctive landing gear pods.
Compared to the ATR-72, the ATR-42 is visibly shorter and has fewer cabin windows.
The nose of the ATR-42 is pointed and sleek for a turboprop aircraft.
The ATR-42's overall appearance is that of a classic, efficient high-wing regional turboprop.
ATR-72: The ATR-72 is a stretched version of the ATR-42, a twin-engine turboprop regional airliner.
Its most defining feature is its long, slender fuselage, which is noticeably longer than the ATR-42.
Like the ATR-42, the ATR-72 features a high-mounted wing and prominent landing gear fairings on the fuselage sides.
This aircraft has a conventional T-tail design.
The ATR-72's two turboprop engines with their six-bladed propellers are mounted on the high wing.
The increased length of the fuselage is the primary way to distinguish an ATR-72 from an ATR-42.
The ATR-72 has a passenger door at the rear of the aircraft, unlike most other airliners.
Its long, thin body combined with the high wing gives the ATR-72 a very distinctive and elegant profile for a turboprop.
The landing gear is housed in the signature ATR side pods, keeping the wing structure clean.
The overall appearance of the ATR-72 is that of a stretched, high-capacity regional turboprop.
An-12: The Antonov An-12 is a four-engine turboprop military transport aircraft with a distinct, vintage Soviet design.
A key identifying feature is its glazed nose navigator station, giving the front of the aircraft a transparent, multi-windowed look.
The An-12 has a high-mounted wing, allowing for a spacious cargo hold accessible via rear loading ramps.
It is equipped with a unique tail gunner position, a transparent bubble at the very rear of the aircraft below the tail.
The aircraft has four powerful Ivchenko AI-20 turboprop engines with large four-bladed propellers.
The main landing gear retracts into large fairings on the sides of the fuselage, similar to the C-130 Hercules.
The tail of the Antonov An-12 is very large and features a tall, single vertical stabilizer.
Its overall shape is robust and functional, designed for carrying heavy cargo into unprepared airfields.
The combination of a glazed nose and a tail gunner position makes the An-12 unmistakable.
The An-12's profile is that of a classic, rugged, four-engine turboprop military transporter.
BAE 146-200: The BAE 146-200 is a four-engine regional jetliner, famous for its unusually high number of engines for its size.
Its most defining characteristic is its four small, quiet high-bypass turbofan engines mounted on pylons under a high wing.
The aircraft features a distinctive T-tail, with a large, swept-back vertical stabilizer.
The high-mounted wing of the BAE 146-200 gives it excellent short-field performance.
The rear of the fuselage features prominent, petal-like airbrakes that open outwards for rapid deceleration.
The BAE 146-200 is the standard-length version of its family.
Its four-engine, high-wing, T-tail configuration is unique among commercial jets and makes it instantly recognizable.
Often nicknamed the 'Jumbolino', the BAE 146-200 looks like a miniature four-engine long-haul jet.
The landing gear retracts into the fuselage, leaving the wings aerodynamically clean.
The combination of four small engines and a T-tail is the unmistakable signature of the BAE 146-200.
BAE 146-300: The BAE 146-300 is the largest and longest variant of the BAE 146 family, a stretched version of the -200.
It is a four-engine, high-wing regional jetliner, and its elongated fuselage is its primary identifier.
Like other 146s, the BAE 146-300 has four small turbofan engines and a prominent T-tail.
The stretched fuselage of the -300 is noticeably longer than the -200, giving it a more slender profile.
It retains the unique clamshell airbrakes at the rear of the fuselage.
The main way to distinguish a BAE 146-300 from other variants is by its greater length and more numerous cabin windows.
The high-wing, four-engine layout is the signature of the BAE 146 family.
The BAE 146-300 does not have the small wingtip fences that were added to its successor, the Avro RJ100.
Its appearance is that of a stretched 'Jumbolino', designed for higher-density regional routes.
The long fuselage combined with the unique four-engine, T-tail configuration makes the BAE 146-300 easy to identify.
BAE-125: The BAE-125, also known as the Hawker 800 series, is a popular mid-sized business jet.
It is a twin-engine jet with a low-mounted, swept-back wing and two engines mounted on the rear fuselage.
The aircraft features a distinctive T-tail configuration.
A key spotting feature of the BAE-125 is its cockpit window design, with five panes including two small triangular 'direct view' windows.
The fuselage of the BAE-125 is sleek and has a circular cross-section, typical of a business jet.
Underneath the fuselage, there are two prominent ventral fins that provide additional stability.
The BAE-125 has a graceful and well-proportioned design, a classic look for a private jet.
Its two turbofan engines are mounted on pylons on either side of the rear fuselage.
Compared to a Learjet, the BAE-125 has a less pointed nose and a more robust overall appearance.
The combination of rear-mounted engines, a T-tail, and distinctive ventral fins makes the BAE-125 identifiable.
Beechcraft 1900: The Beechcraft 1900 is a 19-passenger, twin-engine turboprop airliner designed for regional routes.
It is easily recognized by its long, slender fuselage and its prominent T-tail.
The aircraft has a low-mounted wing with two powerful Pratt & Whitney Canada PT6A turboprop engines.
A unique feature of the Beechcraft 1900 is the presence of small, horizontal fins, called 'tailets', halfway up the vertical stabilizer.
The main landing gear retracts into the engine nacelles.
The fuselage is very long and narrow, giving the aircraft a pencil-like appearance.
The Beechcraft 1900 has a small passenger door at the rear of the fuselage, often with integrated airstairs.
Two large ventral fins are located under the tail for added stability.
The combination of a T-tail with additional 'tailets' is the most definitive spotting feature of the Beechcraft 1900.
Its overall profile is that of a sleek, fast, and long-bodied regional turboprop.
Boeing 717: The Boeing 717 is a twin-engine, single-aisle jetliner designed for the 100-seat market.
It is easily identified by its two powerful, high-mounted engines on the rear fuselage and its T-tail.
The engines of the 717 (Rolls-Royce BR715) are large in diameter and give the aircraft a very powerful appearance.
The Boeing 717 has a relatively short and wide fuselage, a legacy of its origin as the McDonnell Douglas MD-95.
The cockpit of the 717 is a modern glass cockpit, and the exterior has a clean, streamlined look.
Unlike other T-tail jets like the CRJ, the 717 has a wider, 5-abreast fuselage.
The tailcone of the Boeing 717 is pointed and clean, extending aft between the two engines.
Its wings are swept back and feature a modern, efficient design without large winglets.
The combination of large rear-mounted engines and a T-tail is the signature of the Boeing 717.
Its profile is that of a modern, capable, but relatively small mainline jet.
C-130: The Lockheed C-130 Hercules is a versatile four-engine turboprop military transport aircraft.
It is instantly recognizable by its high-mounted, straight wing and four large turboprop engines.
The C-130 has a bulky, cylindrical fuselage designed for carrying large cargo, accessible via a rear loading ramp.
Its main landing gear retracts into large, prominent fairings on the sides of the fuselage.
The tail of the C-130 is very large and tall, with a distinctive fillet at the front of the vertical stabilizer.
The four Allison T56 turboprop engines with their four-bladed propellers produce a characteristic deep, droning sound.
The nose of the C-130 is short and houses the cockpit and, on many variants, a prominent weather radar dome.
The high-wing design allows the fuselage to sit low to the ground, easing cargo loading and unloading.
The C-130's rugged, functional, and iconic shape has remained largely unchanged for decades.
The combination of four turboprops, a high wing, and landing gear pods makes the C-130 Hercules unmistakable.
C-47: The Douglas C-47 Skytrain, also known as the Dakota, is a legendary twin-engine military transport aircraft from the WWII era.
It is identified by its two large radial piston engines, low-mounted wing, and conventional 'tail-dragger' landing gear.
The C-47 has a distinctive tail-down stance on the ground due to its main wheels being forward of the center of gravity and a small tailwheel.
The aircraft has a rounded fuselage with a row of circular cabin windows.
The vertical stabilizer of the C-47 is very large and has a characteristic rounded shape at the top.
The wings of the C-47 have a straight leading edge and a tapered trailing edge.
Its two powerful piston engines, like the Pratt & Whitney R-1830 Twin Wasp, produce a classic, throaty roar.
The C-47's design is iconic, representing a crucial workhorse of the Allied forces.
The combination of radial engines and tail-dragger landing gear is a clear sign of its vintage.
The profile of the Douglas C-47 is one of the most recognizable in aviation history.
CRJ-200: The Bombardier CRJ-200 is a 50-seat regional jet, one of the aircraft that revolutionized regional air travel.
It is identified by its two rear-mounted engines, a high T-tail, and a very narrow, slender fuselage.
The CRJ-200 has a low-mounted wing that is swept back, giving it a sleek appearance.
The windows on the CRJ-200 are noticeably small and are positioned relatively low on the fuselage.
The fuselage itself is narrow, accommodating a 2-2 seating configuration.
The two General Electric CF34 engines are mounted on pylons on the rear fuselage.
The T-tail of the CRJ-200 is very prominent, with sharply swept horizontal stabilizers.
Compared to the larger CRJ-700/900, the CRJ-200 has a shorter fuselage and no leading-edge slats on the wings.
The CRJ-200's profile is that of a small, fast, and efficient regional jet.
Its signature look is the combination of rear-mounted engines, a T-tail, and a long, thin body.
CRJ-700: The Bombardier CRJ-700 is a stretched version of the CRJ-200, designed to carry more passengers.
It is a twin-engine, T-tail regional jet, but its fuselage is significantly longer than the CRJ-200.
A key difference from the CRJ-200 is the presence of leading-edge slats on the wings of the CRJ-700, visible when extended.
The CRJ-700 has a lowered floor compared to the CRJ-200, which results in the cabin windows being positioned higher up on the fuselage.
The longer fuselage of the CRJ-700 gives it a more elegant and proportional look than the shorter -200.
It retains the characteristic rear-mounted engines and high T-tail of the CRJ family.
The wing of the CRJ-700 has a greater span and area than that of the CRJ-200.
From the side, the easiest way to identify a CRJ-700 is its increased length and higher window line.
The CRJ-700 has one or two over-wing emergency exits, depending on the specific configuration.
Its profile is that of a modern, medium-capacity regional jet, larger and more capable than its predecessor.
CRJ-900: The Bombardier CRJ-900 is a further stretch of the CRJ-700, the largest member of the original CRJ family.
Its most prominent feature is its very long and slender fuselage, combined with the signature rear-mounted engines and T-tail.
The CRJ-900 has two over-wing emergency exits on each side, a key feature to distinguish it from the CRJ-700.
It shares the advanced, slatted wing and higher window line of the CRJ-700.
To manage its long fuselage, the CRJ-900 has a slightly larger vertical tail and added ventral fins.
The extreme length-to-width ratio of the CRJ-900's fuselage is its most defining characteristic.
The two General Electric CF34 engines are more powerful versions to accommodate the aircraft's higher weight.
The CRJ-900's profile is that of a high-capacity regional jet, pushing the limits of the original design.
Counting the two over-wing exits is a reliable way to confirm an aircraft is a CRJ-900.
The combination of a very long, thin body, T-tail, and rear engines makes the CRJ-900 an impressive sight.
Cessna 172: The Cessna 172 Skyhawk is one of the most recognizable and popular single-engine general aviation aircraft in the world.
It is a four-seat aircraft identified by its high-wing configuration, with a single wing mounted above the fuselage.
The wings of the Cessna 172 are supported by a single strut on each side running from the fuselage.
It features a tricycle landing gear, with two main wheels and a steerable nose wheel.
The C-172 has a single piston engine at the front, driving a two or three-bladed propeller.
The tail is a conventional design, with a swept-back vertical stabilizer and a straight horizontal stabilizer.
A characteristic feature of later model Cessna 172s is the rear 'Omni-Vision' window, which wraps around the back of the cabin.
The side profile of the Cessna 172, with its high wing and strut, is iconic in the world of personal aviation.
The doors on the C-172 open outwards, providing access to the front and rear seats.
Its overall appearance is simple, reliable, and functional, the very definition of a light training aircraft.
Cessna 208: The Cessna 208 Caravan is a single-engine utility aircraft, known for its versatility and ruggedness.
It is a high-wing turboprop, easily identified by its large, boxy fuselage and a single powerful engine.
The C-208 has a fixed, tricycle landing gear, with the main gear attached to the fuselage via sturdy struts.
A key feature is its single Pratt & Whitney Canada PT6A turboprop engine, which gives it a distinctive sound.
The aircraft's high wing is supported by a single, large strut on each side.
The Cessna 208 is often fitted with a large cargo pod under its fuselage, which is a major spotting feature.
The tail is a large, conventional design to provide stability for the large fuselage.
Its main cabin can be configured for passengers, cargo, or both, with large windows and often a rear cargo door.
The Cessna 208's appearance is purely functional, with a large, spacious body and a powerful engine.
The combination of a single turboprop, high wing, and fixed gear makes the Cessna 208 Caravan unmistakable.
Cessna 525: The Cessna 525 CitationJet (CJ) series is a family of popular light business jets.
It is identified by its two rear-mounted turbofan engines and a straight, unswept wing.
The Cessna 525 features a prominent T-tail, a common design for jets with rear-fuselage engines.
A key feature of the CJ series is its straight, tapered wing, which is unusual for a jet and provides good low-speed handling.
The fuselage is sleek and streamlined, with a circular cross-section and oval cabin windows.
The nose of the Cessna 525 is gently sloped, leading up to a simple, clean cockpit design.
Its two Williams International FJ44 engines are mounted close to the fuselage.
The straight wing and T-tail combination is the most reliable way to identify a Cessna 525.
The landing gear is a simple tricycle arrangement that retracts cleanly into the fuselage and wings.
The overall profile of the Cessna 525 is that of a simple, efficient, and popular entry-level private jet.
Cessna 560: The Cessna 560 Citation V, Ultra, and Encore series are successful mid-sized business jets.
It is a twin-engine jet with a straight wing and a T-tail, similar to the smaller Cessna 525 but larger.
The Cessna 560 can be distinguished from the smaller CJ series by its longer fuselage and greater number of cabin windows.
The aircraft has a low-mounted, straight, tapered wing, a signature of the Citation family.
Its two Pratt & Whitney Canada JT15D or PW500 series engines are mounted on the rear fuselage.
The T-tail of the Cessna 560 is large and well-proportioned for the fuselage size.
The landing gear is a trailing-link type, which provides smoother landings than a simple strut.
The fuselage is longer and more spacious than the entry-level Citation jets.
The Cessna 560's profile is that of a classic, straight-wing Citation, a step up in size and range.
The combination of a straight wing, T-tail, and a mid-sized fuselage makes the Cessna 560 identifiable.
Challenger 600: The Bombardier Challenger 600 series is a family of large business jets known for their very wide, spacious cabin.
A key identifying feature is its wide fuselage, which is noticeably broader than other jets of similar length.
The Challenger 600 has two powerful turbofan engines mounted on the rear fuselage.
It features a distinctive cruciform tail, where the horizontal stabilizers are mounted partway up the vertical fin, not at the top (T-tail).
The aircraft has a low-mounted wing with a supercritical airfoil design and prominent winglets on later variants.
The nose of the Challenger 600 is pointed, and the cockpit has a clean, modern window layout.
The wide fuselage cross-section is the most reliable feature to spot a Challenger 600.
Its two General Electric CF34 engines are the same family used on the CRJ regional jets.
The Challenger 600's profile is that of a powerful, comfortable long-range business jet.
The combination of a very wide body and a cruciform tail is the signature of the Challenger 600 series.
DC-10: The McDonnell Douglas DC-10 is a three-engine, wide-body airliner, a classic from the 1970s.
Its most defining and unmistakable feature is its third engine, which is mounted at the base of the vertical stabilizer and fed by a straight-through duct.
The DC-10 has a wide-body fuselage and two large high-bypass engines mounted on pylons under the wings.
The horizontal stabilizers are mounted low on the rear fuselage, below the #2 engine.
The landing gear of the DC-10 includes a third main gear leg in the center of the fuselage, in addition to the two wing-mounted gears.
The cockpit of the DC-10 has a classic three-crew layout with large windows.
The unique tail-mounted engine design is the definitive way to identify a DC-10.
Compared to the L-1011 TriStar, the DC-10's center engine has a simpler, straight-through design without an S-duct.
Many DC-10s were converted to cargo aircraft (MD-10) and can be seen in freight operations.
The profile of the DC-10, with its two wing engines and one tail engine, is iconic and unique.
DC-3: The Douglas DC-3 is a revolutionary propeller-driven airliner from the 1930s, virtually identical in appearance to the C-47 military transport.
It is a twin-engine aircraft with large radial piston engines and a low-mounted wing.
The DC-3 is a 'tail-dragger', featuring a conventional landing gear with two main wheels at the front and a small tailwheel at the back.
Its characteristic tail-down posture on the ground is a key identifier.
The fuselage is rounded and features a row of rectangular passenger windows.
The vertical tail is very large and has a distinctive rounded leading edge and top.
The wings have a straight leading edge and are tapered on the trailing edge.
The sound of the two Pratt & Whitney R-1830 Twin Wasp radial engines is a deep, unmistakable rumble.
The Douglas DC-3's elegant, vintage lines and tail-dragger stance make it one of the most famous aircraft ever built.
Its combination of radial engines and tailwheel landing gear is a definitive sign of its historic design.
DC-6: The Douglas DC-6 is a large, four-engine piston-powered airliner from the post-WWII era.
It is identified by its four large radial piston engines, each driving a three or four-bladed propeller.
The DC-6 has a long, circular fuselage and a tricycle landing gear, with a nosewheel instead of a tailwheel.
The cockpit of the DC-6 is classic for its time, with numerous small, square windows.
The wings are low-mounted and have a straight, high-aspect-ratio design.
The vertical tail is very tall and has a prominent dorsal fin extending along the top of the fuselage.
The four Pratt & Whitney R-2800 Double Wasp engines give the DC-6 a powerful and imposing presence.
Compared to the DC-3, the DC-6 is much larger, has four engines, and has a nosewheel.
The profile of the Douglas DC-6 is that of a classic, long-range 'propliner' from the golden age of aviation.
The sight and sound of its four massive radial engines is its most defining characteristic.
DC-8: The Douglas DC-8 is a four-engine, narrow-body jet airliner, a contemporary and competitor to the Boeing 707.
It is identified by its four pod-mounted turbojet or low-bypass turbofan engines under a swept wing.
The DC-8 has a long, slender fuselage and a conventional tail.
A key spotting feature of the DC-8 is its distinctive nose, which is more rounded and blunt than the 707's.
The air intakes on the engine pylons of the DC-8 are characteristically large and semi-circular.
The wings of the DC-8 are swept at 30 degrees and lack any wingtip devices.
Later 'Super 60' and 'Super 70' series of the DC-8 are known for their extremely long, stretched fuselages.
The four engines of the DC-8 were later upgraded to quieter, more efficient high-bypass turbofans on the -70 series.
The DC-8's profile is that of a classic, first-generation four-engine jetliner.
The combination of four underwing engines and the rounded nose helps distinguish the DC-8 from the 707.
DC-9-30: The McDonnell Douglas DC-9-30 is a popular variant of the DC-9 twin-engine, single-aisle airliner.
It is easily identified by its two rear-mounted engines and a high T-tail.
A key feature of the DC-9-30 is the presence of prominent leading-edge slats on its wings, which the shorter DC-9-10 lacked.
The fuselage of the -30 variant is stretched compared to the original, giving it a more balanced look.
The DC-9-30 has the classic, pointed 'dolphin' nose and five-pane cockpit window design.
Its two Pratt & Whitney JT8D engines are mounted on pylons on the rear fuselage.
The wings are moderately swept and, unlike later designs, have no winglets.
The profile of the DC-9-30 is that of a classic T-tail jet, a workhorse for many airlines.
The DC-9-30 can be distinguished from the later MD-80 series by its shorter fuselage and smaller engines.
The combination of rear engines, a T-tail, and visible wing slats is characteristic of the DC-9-30.
DH-82: The de Havilland DH-82 Tiger Moth is an iconic two-seat biplane, primarily used as a primary trainer.
Its most defining characteristic is its biplane configuration, with two wings stacked one above the other.
The DH-82 Tiger Moth has an open cockpit for both the student and instructor.
It features a simple, fixed 'tail-dragger' landing gear.
The aircraft is powered by a single de Havilland Gipsy Major inverted inline piston engine at the front, driving a wooden propeller.
The wings of the DH-82 have a noticeable forward sweep on the lower wing and a straight upper wing.
The tail is a simple, fabric-covered structure with external bracing wires.
The sound of its Gipsy Major engine is a distinctive, classic 'putt-putt' of a vintage aircraft.
The Tiger Moth's appearance is the very definition of a classic 1930s training biplane.
Its combination of biplane wings, open cockpits, and tailwheel landing gear makes the DH-82 unmistakable.
DHC-1: The de Havilland Canada DHC-1 Chipmunk is a two-seat, single-engine primary trainer aircraft.
It is a low-wing monoplane with a tandem seating arrangement (one seat behind the other).
A key feature of the DHC-1 is its distinctive sliding canopy, which is heavily framed and covers both cockpits.
The aircraft has a conventional 'tail-dragger' landing gear.
The DHC-1 is powered by a de Havilland Gipsy Major inline piston engine, similar to the Tiger Moth.
The wings are all-metal and have a straight, tapered design.
The vertical tail has a characteristic curved shape, and the rudder is large.
The Chipmunk has a sleek, sporty look for a trainer of its era.
Its all-metal construction and low-wing design represent a step up from the fabric biplanes it replaced.
The DHC-1 Chipmunk's signature look is its tandem cockpit under a long, framed canopy and its classic tail-dragger stance.
DHC-6: The de Havilland Canada DHC-6 Twin Otter is a highly versatile utility aircraft known for its STOL (Short Takeoff and Landing) capabilities.
It is a twin-engine turboprop with a high-mounted, straight, un-swept wing.
A defining feature of the DHC-6 is its boxy, slab-sided fuselage, optimized for cargo and passenger space.
The Twin Otter has a fixed tricycle landing gear, making it rugged and suitable for unprepared runways.
Its two Pratt & Whitney Canada PT6A turboprop engines are mounted on the high wing.
The nose of the DHC-6 is characteristically short and snub-nosed.
The tail is a large, conventional design to ensure stability at low speeds.
The DHC-6 can be fitted with wheels, skis, or floats, making it adaptable to any environment.
Its high wing is supported by a single, prominent strut on each side.
The combination of a high wing, twin turboprops, fixed gear, and a boxy fuselage makes the DHC-6 Twin Otter unmistakable.
DHC-8-100: The de Havilland Canada DHC-8-100 Dash 8 is a twin-engine, high-wing regional turboprop airliner.
It is the original, shortest variant of the Dash 8 family.
The DHC-8-100 is identified by its high-mounted wing, T-tail, and two powerful turboprop engines.
A key feature is its very long engine nacelles, which also house the main landing gear when retracted.
The fuselage of the DHC-8-100 is short and appears robust, giving it a 'stubby' look.
The high T-tail is a signature of the Dash 8 series.
Its two Pratt & Whitney Canada PW100 series engines drive large, multi-bladed propellers.
The combination of a high wing, long engine nacelles, and a T-tail is characteristic of the Dash 8.
Compared to the ATR series, the Dash 8 has a T-tail and the landing gear retracts into the nacelles.
The DHC-8-100's profile is that of a short, powerful, and modern regional turboprop.
DHC-8-300: The de Havilland Canada DHC-8-300 is a stretched version of the Dash 8-100.
It is a high-wing, T-tail, twin-turboprop airliner, but its fuselage is noticeably longer than the -100.
The DHC-8-300 retains the characteristic long engine nacelles that house the landing gear.
The increased length of the fuselage is the primary way to distinguish a DHC-8-300 from a -100.
The wingspan of the DHC-8-300 is also slightly larger than the original model.
It features the same high T-tail and powerful PW100 series engines as the rest of the classic Dash 8 family.
The longer fuselage gives the DHC-8-300 a more slender and elegant profile than the short -100.
The cabin windows are more numerous to accommodate the extra passenger capacity.
The DHC-8-300's signature look is its high-wing, T-tail configuration on a medium-length fuselage.
Visually, the DHC-8-300 is a stretched Dash 8, a common sight on regional routes.
DR-400: The Robin DR-400 is a popular French single-engine light aircraft, widely used in European flying clubs.
Its most defining characteristic is its 'cranked' or 'Jodel' wing design, where the outer portions of the wing have a noticeable dihedral (upward angle).
The DR-400 is a low-wing monoplane with a tricycle landing gear.
It is a wood and fabric aircraft, giving it a very smooth and streamlined appearance.
A key feature is its large, one-piece sliding canopy that provides excellent visibility.
The aircraft has a single piston engine at the front, driving a propeller.
The fixed, spatted landing gear (with fairings over the wheels) is also a common feature.
The DR-400 has a simple, conventional tail.
The cranked wing is the unmistakable signature of the Robin DR-400 and its Jodel predecessors.
Its profile is that of a sleek, efficient, and distinctively-winged European light aircraft.
Dornier 328: The Dornier 328 is a regional airliner that was produced in both turboprop and turbofan versions.
Its most unique feature is its high-mounted wing with a supercritical airfoil, giving it a very modern and fast look for a regional aircraft.
The fuselage of the Dornier 328 is streamlined and not perfectly circular, designed for aerodynamic efficiency.
The turboprop version (328-100) has two Pratt & Whitney Canada PW119 engines with six-bladed propellers.
The landing gear is a tricycle arrangement, with the main gear retracting into fairings on the fuselage sides.
The Dornier 328 has a conventional tail, not a T-tail.
The jet version, the 328JET, has two engines mounted in pods under the wings.
The high wing and fuselage-mounted landing gear give the Dornier 328 a distinctive stance.
Its sleek, advanced wing design is its most prominent and identifiable feature.
The Dornier 328's overall appearance is that of a high-performance, technologically advanced regional transport.
E-170: The Embraer E-170 is a member of the E-Jet family, a modern twin-engine regional jet.
It is identified by its two large, high-bypass engines mounted under a low wing.
A key feature of the E-170 is its distinctive 'double-bubble' fuselage cross-section, which provides a spacious cabin.
The cockpit of the E-170 has a unique, sleek design with four large, prominent windows.
The wings of the E-170 are swept back and feature large, elegant winglets that are integrated into the wing structure.
The E-170 has a conventional tail and a clean, streamlined design.
The nose of the aircraft is sharply pointed, giving it an aggressive, modern look.
As the shortest member of its family, the E-170 has a compact and well-proportioned appearance.
The Embraer E-170's signature look is its combination of a pointed nose, four-pane cockpit, and large integrated winglets.
Its overall profile is that of a state-of-the-art, comfortable regional jet.
E-190: The Embraer E-190 is a stretched version of the E-170, a popular member of the E-Jet family.
It is a twin-engine regional jet, and its fuselage is noticeably longer than the E-170/175.
The E-190 features the same distinctive pointed nose, four-pane cockpit, and integrated winglets as the rest of its family.
A key spotting difference is the presence of two over-wing emergency exits on the E-190, whereas the E-170/175 has only one.
The longer fuselage gives the E-190 a very sleek and elegant side profile.
Its two General Electric CF34 engines are mounted in pods under the low-mounted wing.
The E-190 has a conventional tail design and the 'double-bubble' fuselage.
The combination of the Embraer family nose and the two over-wing exits is the definitive way to identify an E-190.
The E-190's appearance is that of a larger, mainline-capable regional jet.
Its balanced, stretched proportions and signature winglets make the Embraer E-190 a very modern-looking aircraft.
E-195: The Embraer E-195 is the longest variant of the first-generation E-Jet family, a further stretch of the E-190.
Its most defining characteristic is its long, slender fuselage, making it the highest-capacity E-Jet.
The E-195 shares the family's signature pointed nose, four-pane cockpit, and integrated winglets.
Like the E-190, the E-195 also has two over-wing emergency exits on each side.
Distinguishing an E-195 from an E-190 visually can be difficult, as it relies on judging the subtle extra length of the fuselage.
The wings and engines of the E-195 are largely the same as the E-190, but the structure is strengthened for the higher weight.
The E-195's long fuselage gives it a very graceful, almost glider-like appearance.
The profile of the Embraer E-195 is that of the ultimate stretch of the E-Jet design.
The combination of the Embraer family look with the longest fuselage is the key to identifying an E-195.
Its appearance is that of a narrow-body jet that bridges the gap between regional and mainline aircraft.
EMB-120: The Embraer EMB-120 Brasilia is a twin-engine turboprop regional airliner known for its speed.
It is easily identified by its low-mounted wing and a high T-tail.
The EMB-120 has a streamlined, circular fuselage and a sleek, pointed nose.
Its two powerful Pratt & Whitney Canada PW118 turboprop engines are mounted in nacelles on the low wing.
The main landing gear of the EMB-120 retracts into the engine nacelles.
The high T-tail is a very prominent feature of its design.
The EMB-120 has a fast, modern look for a turboprop, with swept-back wings and tail surfaces.
Its side profile is that of a sleek, corporate-style turboprop transport.
Compared to other turboprops like the Saab 340, the EMB-120 has a T-tail.
The combination of a low wing and a T-tail is the signature look of the Embraer EMB-120.
ERJ 135: The Embraer ERJ 135 is a regional jet, the shortest member of the ERJ family.
It is a twin-engine jet with rear-mounted engines and a high T-tail.
The ERJ 135 has a very narrow, circular fuselage with a 1-2 seating arrangement.
A key feature of the ERJ family is the wing design, which lacks leading-edge slats and has swept-back wings.
The ERJ 135's short fuselage gives it a compact and sporty appearance.
Its two Rolls-Royce AE 3007 engines are mounted on pylons on the rear fuselage.
The T-tail is sharply swept and prominent.
The ERJ 135 can be distinguished from the longer ERJ 145 by its shorter length and fewer cabin windows.
The ERJ 135's profile is that of a small, fast, and efficient 'jet-powered tube'.
Its signature look is the combination of rear engines, T-tail, and a very slender fuselage.
ERJ 145: The Embraer ERJ 145 is the baseline and most common model of the ERJ regional jet family.
It is a twin-engine, T-tail jet, with its fuselage stretched compared to the ERJ 135.
The ERJ 145 has a long, extremely slender 'pencil-like' fuselage, its most defining characteristic.
The aircraft has two Rolls-Royce AE 3007 engines mounted on the rear fuselage.
The wings are low-mounted, swept back, and often feature small, simple winglets.
The high T-tail is a signature of the ERJ family.
The ERJ 145's extreme length-to-width ratio gives it a very distinctive side profile.
Compared to the CRJ series, the ERJ 145 has a narrower fuselage and a different wing and tail design.
The long, thin fuselage combined with the T-tail and rear engines is the unmistakable look of the ERJ 145.
Its appearance is that of a stretched, high-speed regional jet, a common sight on short-haul routes.
Embraer Legacy 600: The Embraer Legacy 600 is a large business jet based on the ERJ 135 regional jet airframe.
It is a twin-engine jet with rear-mounted engines and a high T-tail.
A key identifying feature of the Legacy 600 is the addition of large, prominent winglets, which the standard ERJ family lacks.
The aircraft also has a large ventral fuel tank fairing under the center fuselage, which increases its range.
The fuselage of the Legacy 600 is the same size as the ERJ 135, but it has fewer, larger, and more oval-shaped cabin windows.
The combination of the ERJ airframe with large winglets is the definitive signature of the Legacy 600.
It retains the T-tail and rear-mounted Rolls-Royce engines of its airliner sibling.
The Legacy 600's profile is that of a long-range, spacious business jet.
The large winglets and the under-belly fuel tank are the best ways to distinguish it from an ERJ 135.
Its appearance is a blend of a regional jet's ruggedness with the luxury features of a private jet.
Eurofighter Typhoon: The Eurofighter Typhoon is a modern twin-engine, canard-delta wing multirole fighter jet.
Its most defining feature is its delta wing combined with a large, forward-mounted canard.
The Typhoon has two powerful Eurojet EJ200 engines located side-by-side in the rear fuselage.
It has a single, large vertical stabilizer.
The air intake is a large, rectangular 'chin' intake located below the cockpit.
The cockpit features a large, single-piece bubble canopy providing excellent visibility.
The canard-delta configuration gives the Eurofighter Typhoon extreme agility and a highly recognizable silhouette.
The aircraft has a sleek, futuristic design built for speed and maneuverability.
From below, the large delta wing and rectangular intake are the clearest identifiers.
The Typhoon's overall appearance is that of a cutting-edge, agile European fighter aircraft.
F-16A/B: The General Dynamics F-16A/B Fighting Falcon is a single-engine, multirole fighter jet, one of the most common fighters in the world.
Its most iconic feature is the large bubble canopy, which provides outstanding visibility for the pilot.
The F-16A/B has a single, powerful turbofan engine fed by a prominent, underslung 'chin' air intake.
It features a blended wing-body design, where the wings smoothly merge into the fuselage for better aerodynamics.
The F-16 has a single vertical stabilizer and two ventral fins under the tail.
The wings are moderately swept and have missile rails on the wingtips.
The F-16A is the single-seat version, while the F-16B is the two-seat trainer with a longer canopy.
The aircraft's sleek, aggressive lines and bubble canopy make it instantly recognizable.
From below, the single engine exhaust and chin intake are key identifiers.
The F-16A/B's profile is that of a classic, agile, and highly successful modern fighter jet.
F/A-18: The McDonnell Douglas F/A-18 Hornet is a twin-engine, multirole fighter jet, primarily operated by naval forces.
Its most defining characteristic is its twin vertical stabilizers that are canted outwards.
The F/A-18 has two engines, fed by two rectangular air intakes located on the sides of the fuselage under the wing leading edge extensions (LEX).
The wings are moderately swept and feature a prominent 'dogtooth' notch on the leading edge.
The Hornet has large, distinctive leading-edge root extensions (LEX) that run alongside the cockpit.
The landing gear is very robust, designed for carrier landings, with a prominent two-wheel nose gear.
The F/A-18 has a single-piece bubble canopy on the single-seat version and a longer canopy on the two-seat version.
The twin outward-canted tails are the most reliable way to identify an F/A-18.
Its profile is that of a rugged, versatile, and capable carrier-based fighter.
The combination of twin tails, side intakes, and large LEXs gives the F/A-18 a unique and powerful look.
Falcon 2000: The Dassault Falcon 2000 is a large-cabin, transcontinental business jet.
It is a twin-engine jet with two rear-mounted engines and a conventional, swept-back tail.
A key feature of the Falcon 2000 is its two powerful Pratt & Whitney Canada PW308C engines in sleek nacelles.
The aircraft has a low-mounted wing with a highly optimized, advanced airfoil design, often with winglets.
The Falcon 2000 has a wide and spacious fuselage, derived from the larger Falcon 900.
Unlike the Falcon 900, the Falcon 2000 is a twinjet, not a trijet.
The cockpit windows have a distinctive, elegant shape, characteristic of Dassault aircraft.
The Falcon 2000's profile is that of a modern, efficient, and luxurious long-range business jet.
It has a clean and uncluttered design, with a simple and elegant tail.
The combination of two rear engines, a conventional tail, and the signature Falcon nose makes the 2000 identifiable.
Falcon 900: The Dassault Falcon 900 is a large-cabin, long-range trijet business aircraft.
Its most defining and unmistakable feature is its trijet engine configuration, with two engines on the rear fuselage and a central engine at the base of the tail.
The central engine of the Falcon 900 is fed by an S-shaped duct, similar to the Boeing 727.
The aircraft has a low-mounted, swept-back wing, often with blended winglets.
The fuselage of the Falcon 900 is wide and provides a very spacious cabin.
The trijet layout is a signature of Dassault's long-range Falcon jets and makes the 900 instantly recognizable.
The cockpit and nose section have the sleek, elegant design characteristic of the Falcon family.
The Falcon 900 has a conventional tail, with the third engine integrated into the vertical stabilizer.
Its three-engine design is unique among modern business jets.
The Falcon 900's silhouette, with its two side engines and one tail engine, is iconic.
Fokker 100: The Fokker 100 is a twin-engine, single-aisle regional jetliner.
It is easily identified by its two rear-mounted Rolls-Royce Tay engines and a high T-tail.
A key feature of the Fokker 100 is its long, slender fuselage, giving it a 'pencil-like' appearance.
The tailcone of the Fokker 100 is unique, splitting into two petal-like sections that act as an airbrake.
The wings are low-mounted, moderately swept, and have a very clean, efficient design.
The Fokker 100 has a distinctive five-abreast seating arrangement, resulting in a slightly wider fuselage than some competitors.
The cockpit has a classic design with large window panes.
The combination of rear engines, a T-tail, and the unique split-tail airbrake is the definitive signature of the Fokker 100.
Its two Tay engines are relatively quiet and efficient low-bypass turbofans.
The profile of the Fokker 100 is that of a sleek, quiet, and well-designed European regional jet.
Fokker 50: The Fokker 50 is a twin-engine turboprop regional airliner, a modernized version of the F27 Friendship.
It is a high-wing aircraft with two powerful Pratt & Whitney Canada PW125B engines driving six-bladed propellers.
The Fokker 50 has a long, circular fuselage and a conventional tail.
A key feature is its long, slender engine nacelles which also house the main landing gear.
The high wing allows for excellent ground clearance and provides a stable ride.
The six-bladed propellers on the Fokker 50 are a good identifier, as they are more numerous than on many older turboprops.
The fuselage sits relatively low to the ground, a characteristic of its high-wing design.
The Fokker 50 has a clean and modern look for a turboprop of its generation.
Its profile is that of a classic, efficient, high-wing regional airliner.
The combination of a high wing, conventional tail, and long landing gear nacelles makes the Fokker 50 recognizable.
Fokker 70: The Fokker 70 is a shortened version of the Fokker 100 regional jet.
It is a twin-engine, T-tail jet, and its primary identifier is its short fuselage.
Like the Fokker 100, the Fokker 70 features the unique split-tailcone airbrake.
The aircraft has two rear-mounted Rolls-Royce Tay engines.
The short fuselage of the Fokker 70 gives it a more compact and 'stubby' appearance compared to the long F100.
