The wings and tail are largely the same as the Fokker 100.
To differentiate a Fokker 70 from a Fokker 100, one must judge the shorter length and fewer cabin windows.
The combination of the Fokker T-tail design and the tailcone airbrake on a short body is the signature of the Fokker 70.
Its profile is that of a smaller, but equally capable, version of the Fokker 100.
The Fokker 70's unique design features make it easy to identify as a member of the Fokker jet family.
Global Express: The Bombardier Global Express is a family of large-cabin, ultra-long-range business jets.
It is a twin-engine jet with a low-mounted wing and a prominent T-tail.
A key feature of the Global Express is its very long and sleek fuselage, designed for intercontinental flights.
The wings have a highly advanced, supercritical design and are equipped with distinctive winglets.
The two powerful Rolls-Royce BR710 engines are mounted on the rear fuselage.
The T-tail is very tall and swept back, giving the aircraft a majestic appearance.
The Global Express has a characteristicly long cabin with numerous large, oval windows.
The cockpit and nose section are very streamlined and modern.
Its profile is that of a top-tier, high-performance business jet, built for speed and range.
The combination of a long fuselage, advanced wing with winglets, and a large T-tail defines the look of the Global Express.
Gulfstream IV: The Gulfstream IV is a popular large-cabin, long-range business jet.
It is a twin-engine jet with a low-mounted wing, two rear-mounted engines, and a T-tail.
A signature feature of the Gulfstream IV (and all Gulfstreams) is its large, distinctive oval cabin windows.
The G-IV has a long, elegant fuselage and a clean, aerodynamic design.
Its two Rolls-Royce Tay engines are the same type used on the Fokker 100.
The wings are swept back and, unlike later Gulfstreams, the G-IV typically does not have winglets.
The high T-tail is a classic feature of rear-engine jet designs.
The profile of the Gulfstream IV is that of a classic, luxurious private jet, a symbol of corporate aviation.
The large oval windows are the most reliable way to identify an aircraft as a Gulfstream.
The combination of the Gulfstream windows, rear engines, and T-tail makes the G-IV easy to recognize.
Gulfstream V: The Gulfstream V is an ultra-long-range business jet, a larger and more capable successor to the G-IV.
It is a twin-engine, T-tail jet, but it is noticeably larger than the Gulfstream IV.
The G-V is powered by two large, powerful Rolls-Royce BR710 engines, bigger than the Tay engines on the G-IV.
A key difference is the longer fuselage and greater wingspan of the Gulfstream V.
The G-V retains the signature large, oval cabin windows of the Gulfstream family.
The wings of the Gulfstream V are a new, advanced design for long-range efficiency, and it lacks winglets.
The T-tail is very large and tall, in proportion to the larger airframe.
The Gulfstream V's profile is that of a very long-range, high-performance intercontinental business jet.
Its larger size and more powerful engines are the primary visual cues to distinguish it from a G-IV.
The combination of massive engines, a long fuselage, and the iconic oval windows defines the Gulfstream V.
Hawk T1: The BAE Systems Hawk T1 is a single-engine, tandem-seat advanced jet trainer.
It is a small, sleek jet with a low-mounted, swept-back wing.
A key feature of the Hawk T1 is its single Rolls-Royce Adour turbofan engine, with two small, semi-circular air intakes on the fuselage sides.
The aircraft has a tandem cockpit, with two seats one behind the other under a long, single-piece canopy.
The tail is a conventional design, with a prominent, swept vertical stabilizer.
The Hawk T1 has a slightly 'humped' appearance due to the raised rear cockpit.
The wings are moderately swept and have a simple, clean design.
The Red Arrows, the RAF's aerobatic team, famously fly the Hawk T1, making its shape highly recognizable in that livery.
The Hawk T1 has a simple, tricycle landing gear.
Its profile is that of a classic, agile, and highly successful jet trainer.
Il-76: The Ilyushin Il-76 is a large, four-engine strategic airlifter with a classic Soviet design.
It is easily identified by its high-mounted, swept-back wing and four large turbofan engines.
A signature feature of the Il-76 is its extensively glazed nose, which houses the navigator's station.
The aircraft has a prominent T-tail, with the horizontal stabilizers mounted on top of a very tall vertical fin.
The main landing gear of the Il-76 is very robust, with multi-wheel bogies that retract into large fuselage fairings.
The rear of the fuselage features large clamshell doors for cargo loading, and many military versions have a tail gunner position.
The four Soloviev D-30 turbofan engines are mounted on pylons under the wing.
The combination of a high wing, four engines, a T-tail, and a glazed nose makes the Il-76 unmistakable.
The Il-76 has a rugged, powerful appearance, designed for operating in harsh conditions.
Its unique silhouette is a hallmark of Soviet-era heavy transport aircraft design.
L-1011: The Lockheed L-1011 TriStar is a three-engine, wide-body airliner, a contemporary of the DC-10.
Its most defining feature is its third engine, which is mounted in the tail and fed by a distinctive S-shaped air duct.
The S-duct intake for the #2 engine is located at the base of the vertical stabilizer, giving it a very smooth, integrated look.
The L-1011 has two large high-bypass Rolls-Royce RB211 engines mounted under the wings.
The horizontal stabilizers are mounted low on the rear fuselage, below the central engine exhaust.
The TriStar has a wide-body fuselage and a clean, advanced wing for its time.
The elegant S-duct is the key feature that distinguishes the L-1011 from the DC-10's straight-through tail engine.
The L-1011 has a reputation for being technologically advanced, with features like an autoland system.
The cockpit has large windows and a classic, wide-body layout.
The unique and graceful S-duct tail engine design makes the L-1011 TriStar an iconic and easily identifiable aircraft.
MD-11: The McDonnell Douglas MD-11 is a three-engine, wide-body airliner, a modernized and stretched successor to the DC-10.
It retains the classic trijet layout, with two wing engines and a third engine mounted in the tail.
A key identifying feature of the MD-11 is the presence of prominent winglets on its wingtips, which the DC-10 lacked.
The fuselage of the MD-11 is longer than the DC-10, and it has a smaller horizontal stabilizer.
The #2 engine in the tail has a slightly smaller, more refined intake compared to the DC-10.
The MD-11 has a modern, two-crew glass cockpit.
The combination of the DC-10's three-engine layout with the addition of winglets is the unmistakable signature of the MD-11.
Many MD-11s are now used as cargo freighters, recognizable by their windowless fuselage.
The aircraft's profile is that of a stretched, updated classic, bridging the gap to modern twinjets.
The winglets are the quickest and most reliable way to distinguish an MD-11 from its predecessor, the DC-10.
MD-80: The McDonnell Douglas MD-80 series is a family of twin-engine, single-aisle airliners, developed from the DC-9.
It is identified by its two rear-mounted engines, a high T-tail, and a very long, slender fuselage.
The MD-80 has a much longer fuselage than the DC-9, giving it a distinctive 'long tube' appearance.
The engines on the MD-80 are quieter and more efficient high-bypass Pratt & Whitney JT8D-200 series.
The tailcone of the MD-80 is characteristically pointed or sometimes 'screwdriver-shaped'.
The wings of the MD-80 are similar to the DC-9 but have an increased span.
The cockpit retains the classic five-pane 'dolphin nose' design of the DC-9.
The long fuselage combined with the T-tail and rear engines is the signature look of the MD-80.
Compared to a Boeing 737, the MD-80 has no engines under the wings and a T-tail.
Its profile is that of a stretched, classic T-tail jetliner, a common sight throughout the 1980s and 90s.
MD-87: The McDonnell Douglas MD-87 is a shortened-fuselage variant of the MD-80.
It combines the modern features of the MD-80, like its efficient engines, with a fuselage length similar to the old DC-9-30.
A key identifying feature of the MD-87 is its taller, more angular vertical stabilizer compared to other MD-80s.
The short fuselage combined with the large, powerful engines of the MD-80 series gives the MD-87 a sporty, powerful look.
Like other MD-80s, the MD-87 has a T-tail and two rear-mounted engines.
The pointed tailcone is another characteristic feature.
The MD-87's short body and tall tail make its profile distinct from the much longer standard MD-80 variants.
It is a relatively rare aircraft compared to the other members of the MD-80 family.
The combination of a short body with the advanced features and taller tail of the late MD-80 series is the key to identifying an MD-87.
Its appearance is that of a compact, high-performance T-tail jet.
MD-90: The McDonnell Douglas MD-90 is a further development of the MD-80 series, featuring new, quieter engines.
It is a twin-engine, T-tail jet, but it is powered by two large, modern IAE V2500 high-bypass turbofan engines.
These engines are noticeably larger in diameter and quieter than the JT8D engines on the MD-80.
The MD-90 has a fuselage length similar to the MD-88, one of the longer MD-80 variants.
The combination of the classic MD-80 T-tail airframe with the new, large-diameter engines is the key identifier for the MD-90.
The aircraft features a modern glass cockpit.
The profile of the MD-90 is that of the ultimate evolution of the DC-9 lineage.
The larger engines are the most reliable visual cue to distinguish an MD-90 from an MD-80.
The tailcone is typically pointed, and the cockpit retains the classic 'dolphin nose'.
The MD-90's appearance is a blend of a classic design with modern, efficient powerplant technology.
Metroliner: The Fairchild Swearingen Metroliner is a 19-seat, twin-turboprop airliner, known for its very long, slender fuselage.
Its most defining characteristic is its extremely high length-to-width ratio, giving it a 'flying pencil' or 'sky-cigar' look.
The Metroliner has a low-mounted wing and a conventional tail.
The aircraft is powered by two Garrett TPE331 turboprop engines, which produce a distinctive high-pitched sound.
The cabin windows on the Metroliner are small and circular.
The nose is long and pointed, adding to the aircraft's sleek appearance.
The landing gear is a simple tricycle arrangement.
The extremely long and thin fuselage is the most unmistakable feature of the Metroliner.
The tail is swept back and relatively large to control the long body.
Its profile is unique and easily recognizable among regional turboprops due to its extreme proportions.
Model B200: The Beechcraft Super King Air B200 is a highly popular twin-turboprop utility and executive transport aircraft.
Its most defining characteristic is its high T-tail, a signature of the King Air family.
The Model B200 has a low-mounted wing with two powerful Pratt & Whitney Canada PT6A turboprop engines.
The fuselage is pressurized and features distinctive, long, squared-off cabin windows.
The main landing gear retracts forward into the engine nacelles.
The King Air B200 has a classic, robust design that is trusted worldwide.
The T-tail is the most reliable feature for identifying a King Air.
The squared windows are another key feature that distinguishes it from other similar-sized aircraft.
The profile of the Model B200 is that of a versatile, high-performance turboprop, at home on both paved runways and rough strips.
The combination of a T-tail, low wing, and squared windows makes the Super King Air B200 easy to identify.
PA-28: The Piper PA-28 Cherokee series is a family of popular single-engine light aircraft for training and personal use.
It is easily identified by its low-wing configuration, a signature of most Piper aircraft.
The PA-28 has a single door, located on the co-pilot's (right) side of the fuselage, which passengers and crew use to climb over the wing.
The aircraft has a fixed tricycle landing gear, with spats (fairings) often covering the wheels.
It is powered by a single Lycoming piston engine driving a propeller.
The tail is a conventional design, with a distinctive swept-forward vertical stabilizer on many models.
The wing of the PA-28 is a constant-chord 'Hershey Bar' wing on early models, or a tapered wing on later models like the Archer.
The low-wing design and single passenger door are the most reliable spotting features.
The PA-28's profile is that of a classic, stable, and simple light aircraft.
Its appearance is a staple at general aviation airports around the world.
SR-20: The Cirrus SR-20 is a modern, single-engine, composite light aircraft.
Its most defining characteristic is its sleek, all-composite fuselage with no rivets or seams.
The SR-20 has a low-mounted wing that features a distinctive, continuous curve on the leading edge.
A key safety feature, though not always visible, is the Cirrus Airframe Parachute System (CAPS), housed in the rear fuselage.
The aircraft has a fixed tricycle landing gear with distinctive aerodynamic fairings.
The SR-20 has a side-stick control system instead of a traditional yoke, and a large glass cockpit display.
The tail is a conventional design, but it is also made of smooth composite materials.
The cabin is accessed by two upward-swinging gull-wing doors.
The SR-20's appearance is very modern and streamlined, representing a new generation of personal aircraft.
The smooth composite construction and unique wing shape make the Cirrus SR-20 easily recognizable.
Saab 2000: The Saab 2000 is a high-speed, twin-engine regional turboprop airliner.
Its most defining feature is its extremely long and slender fuselage, combined with a very large and powerful engine installation.
The Saab 2000 is one of the fastest turboprops ever built, and its sleek design reflects this.
It has a low-mounted wing and a conventional tail.
The two Allison AE 2100 turboprop engines drive slow-turning, six-bladed propellers, which are located far from the fuselage to reduce cabin noise.
The main landing gear retracts into the greatly extended engine nacelles.
The vertical tail is very tall to handle the long fuselage.
The Saab 2000's profile is that of a stretched, high-performance 'Concorde of turboprops'.
The combination of a very long, thin body and large, powerful six-bladed propellers is its signature.
Its appearance is unique, blending the size of a regional jet with the efficiency of a turboprop.
Saab 340: The Saab 340 is a popular twin-engine turboprop regional airliner.
It is a low-wing monoplane with a conventional tail, a classic layout for an aircraft of its class.
The Saab 340 is powered by two General Electric CT7 turboprop engines.
A key feature is its retractable tricycle landing gear, with the main gear retracting into the engine nacelles.
The fuselage is unpressurized on early models and pressurized on later ones, with a circular cross-section.
The tail has a large dorsal fin that extends along the top of the rear fuselage.
The Saab 340 has a clean and efficient design, a workhorse of regional aviation.
Compared to the EMB-120, the Saab 340 has a conventional tail instead of a T-tail.
The aircraft's profile is well-proportioned and straightforward.
The combination of a low wing, conventional tail, and nacelle-mounted landing gear defines the look of the Saab 340.
Spitfire: The Supermarine Spitfire is a legendary British single-seat fighter aircraft from WWII.
Its most defining characteristic is its beautiful, elliptical wing shape, which is unique among fighter aircraft.
The Spitfire is a single-engine aircraft, powered by the famous Rolls-Royce Merlin or Griffon V12 piston engine.
It has a sleek, streamlined fuselage and a conventional tail with a rounded vertical stabilizer.
The cockpit is covered by a framed canopy, with many variants featuring a rear-view 'bubble' canopy.
The Spitfire has a narrow-track, retractable conventional landing gear.
The sound of its Merlin engine is one of the most evocative and recognizable sounds in aviation.
The elliptical wing is the most reliable and iconic feature for identifying a Spitfire.
The aircraft's graceful curves and powerful engine give it an appearance of both elegance and lethality.
The profile of the Spitfire is one of the most famous and beloved in the history of flight.
Tornado: The Panavia Tornado is a twin-engine, variable-sweep wing multirole combat aircraft.
Its most defining and unique feature is its variable-geometry or 'swing' wings, which can be swept back for high-speed flight or moved forward for low-speed handling.
The Tornado has two powerful Turbo-Union RB199 engines mounted side-by-side in the rear fuselage.
It features a single, very tall vertical stabilizer.
The aircraft has a tandem cockpit for a pilot and navigator, under a long, single-piece canopy.
The air intakes are two large, rectangular intakes located on the sides of the fuselage.
The Tornado is equipped with a thrust reverser system, with visible 'buckets' that deploy over the engine exhausts.
The swing-wing capability makes the Tornado's silhouette highly variable and unmistakable.
Its profile is that of a rugged, low-level strike aircraft, built to be fast and stable.
The combination of swing wings, a tall single tail, and side-by-side engines defines the Panavia Tornado.
Tu-134: The Tupolev Tu-134 is a twin-engine, narrow-body jetliner from the Soviet era, known for its distinctive glazed nose.
It is easily identified by its two rear-mounted engines and a high T-tail.
The most unique feature of the Tu-134 is its glass 'navigator's nose cone', which is transparent on most early models.
The aircraft has sharply swept-back wings, designed for high-speed flight.
The main landing gear retracts into prominent fairings on the trailing edge of the wings.
The two Soloviev D-30 engines are mounted on pylons on the rear fuselage.
The Tu-134 has a sleek, almost fighter-like appearance for an airliner, with its pointed nose and swept wings.
The glazed nose is the most reliable way to identify a Tu-134.
Its profile is that of a classic, first-generation Soviet T-tail jet.
The combination of a T-tail, swept wings, and a glass nose makes the Tu-134 a unique and memorable aircraft.
Tu-154: The Tupolev Tu-154 is a three-engine, medium-range narrow-body airliner, the workhorse of Soviet and Russian airlines for decades.
It has a trijet configuration similar to the Boeing 727, with two engines on the rear fuselage and a central engine in the tail.
A key spotting feature of the Tu-154 is its main landing gear, which consists of multi-wheel bogies that retract into distinctive wing pods.
The wings are sharply swept back at 35 degrees, giving it a high-speed appearance.
The tail is a T-tail design, with the horizontal stabilizers mounted above the central engine.
The Tu-154 sits noticeably low to the ground.
The large wing pods for the landing gear are a unique and reliable identifier.
Compared to the 727, the Tu-154 has a different landing gear arrangement and a wider fuselage.
The Tu-154's profile is that of a large, fast, and powerful trijet, a symbol of Soviet civil aviation.
The combination of a T-tail trijet layout and the large landing gear pods makes the Tu-154 unmistakable.
Yak-42: The Yakovlev Yak-42 is a three-engine, medium-range airliner, a Soviet-era design intended to replace the Tu-134.
It features a trijet layout with two rear-mounted engines and a central engine fed by an S-shaped duct, similar to the L-1011.
The Yak-42 has a conventional tail, not a T-tail, with the horizontal stabilizers mounted on the fuselage.
The wings are moderately swept and have a clean design.
A unique feature of the Yak-42 is the retractable airstair at the rear of the fuselage, under the tail.
The S-duct for the central engine gives the tail a very smooth and integrated appearance.
The main landing gear retracts into the wing roots.
The Yak-42's profile is that of a robust and capable medium-haul jet.
The combination of a trijet layout with an S-duct and a conventional tail is the key to identifying a Yak-42.
Its appearance is somewhat of a mix between a Boeing 727 (trijet) and a classic twinjet (conventional tail).
